hops.context: A typical example for an Industrial Ethernet protocol is PROFINET I/O [16] which contains three traffic classes, isochronous real-time (IRT), real-time (RT), and non real-time (NRT).
Depending on the required real-time class, proper “Industrial Ethernet”-protocols evolved, e.g., communication protocols addressing real-time class C implement a modified MAC-layer [15].

question: In the context of PROFINET I/O as a part of Industrial Ethernet, which of the following best describes the optimization of communication protocols addressing Real Time Class C?

options.A: These protocols mostly enhance security features without modifying the MAC-layer, suitable for cycle times above 5 milliseconds.
options.B: These protocols implement a modified MAC-layer to support cycle times less than 1 millisecond.
options.C: They rely on unmodified MAC-layers to maintain standard Ethernet transmission speeds, irrespective of cycle times.
options.D: They focus on enhancing data rate by utilizing standard MAC-layers suitable for cycle times greater than 10 milliseconds.

explanation: Real Time Class C in the context of PROFINET I/O, which is optimized for Industrial Ethernet, has a cycle time of less than 1 ms. The modification of the MAC-layer in communication protocols addressing Real Time Class C is specifically tailored to meet these stringent time requirements, distinguishing it from other classes that might not require such rapid processing speeds.
